 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.15.1","title":"TEST: Store/Placement/Repair Simulation + Bounds","description":"# TEST: Store/Placement/Repair Simulation + Bounds

## Goal
Make offline resilience measurable and bounded. Prove that the store layer maintains availability guarantees.

## Test Categories

### Object Store Tests
- [ ] Object write produces correct ObjectId (keyed derivation)
- [ ] Object read returns identical bytes
- [ ] Missing object returns appropriate error
- [ ] Object metadata (zone, schema, retention) is preserved
- [ ] Object reference counting works correctly
- [ ] Object deletion respects GC roots

### Symbol Store Tests
- [ ] Symbols stored with correct ESI mapping
- [ ] Symbol retrieval by ESI works
- [ ] Symbol availability tracking is accurate
- [ ] Symbol eviction respects retention class
- [ ] Quarantine zone isolation

### Placement Policy Tests
- [ ] Coverage evaluation correctness (basis points calculation)
- [ ] min_coverage threshold enforcement
- [ ] max_concentration limit enforcement (no single-source)
- [ ] Geographic diversity when node location available
- [ ] Placement decisions are deterministic (same inputs → same placement)

### Background Repair Loop Tests
- [ ] Repair loop improves coverage over time (measurable)
- [ ] Repair respects bandwidth budget
- [ ] Repair prioritizes objects below threshold
- [ ] Repair actions produce audit events
- [ ] Repair loop is idempotent (no duplicate work)

### Retention/GC Tests
- [ ] GC roots (ZoneCheckpoint) prevent premature eviction
- [ ] Retention class ordering: Required > Pinned > Cached > Ephemeral
- [ ] Ephemeral objects evicted first under pressure
- [ ] GC produces audit events
- [ ] No orphaned symbols after GC

### ChunkedObjectManifest Tests
- [ ] Large object → manifest + chunks
- [ ] Chunk placement distributed correctly
- [ ] Manifest references valid chunk ObjectIds
- [ ] Reassembly produces original object
- [ ] Partial chunk availability tracked correctly

### Quarantine Tests
- [ ] Quarantine eviction order matches policy (by age, by risk)
- [ ] Quarantined objects isolated from normal storage
- [ ] Quarantine entry produces audit event
- [ ] Quarantine promotion/demotion paths

## Simulation Framework
```rust
#[test]
fn test_repair_improves_coverage() {
    let harness = StoreSimulation::new()
        .with_nodes(5)
        .with_failure_rate(0.1);
    
    harness.store_object(test_object, min_coverage: 5000);
    harness.fail_node(2);
    
    assert!(harness.coverage() < 5000);
    harness.run_repair_loop(Duration::from_secs(10));
    assert!(harness.coverage() >= 5000);
}
```

## Golden Vectors
- Object storage: content → ObjectId mapping
- Placement decisions: node set → placement result
- Coverage calculation: symbol distribution → basis points

## Logging Requirements
All store tests MUST emit structured JSON:
```json
{
  "timestamp": "RFC3339",
  "test_name": "test_repair_improves_coverage",
  "module": "fcp-store",
  "phase": "execute|verify",
  "operation": "write|read|repair|gc|placement",
  "object_id": "...",
  "object_size": 12345,
  "symbol_count": 13,
  "coverage_bps": 7500,
  "nodes_holding": ["node-0", "node-2", "node-4"],
  "result": "pass|fail",
  "duration_us": 1234
}
```

### For Repair Actions
```json
{
  "repair_action": "replicate",
  "object_id": "...",
  "source_node": "node-0",
  "target_node": "node-3",
  "coverage_before_bps": 4000,
  "coverage_after_bps": 6000,
  "reason_code": "BELOW_THRESHOLD"
}
```

### For GC Events
```json
{
  "gc_action": "evict",
  "object_id": "...",
  "retention_class": "Ephemeral",
  "reason": "SPACE_PRESSURE",
  "gc_root_checked": true
}
```

## Determinism Requirements
- Simulation tests MUST use seeded RNG
- All tests MUST be reproducible from seed
- No flaky tests allowed

## Acceptance Criteria
- Simulation tests run deterministically
- Repair loop measurably improves coverage
- GC respects retention and roots
- All operations produce structured logs

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.16","title":"[FCP2] Tailscale Integration: MeshIdentity, NodeKeyAttestation, Peer Discovery, ACL Tag Mapping","description":"# [FCP2] Tailscale Integration: MeshIdentity, NodeKeyAttestation, Peer Discovery, ACL Tag Mapping

## Goal
Abstract Tailscale as the authenticated mesh substrate.

## Required behaviors
### MeshIdentity model
MeshIdentity MUST include:
- tailscale node_id
- hostname
- IPs
- ACL tags
- owner_pubkey anchor
- node signing/encryption/issuance pubkeys + kids
- owner-signed NodeKeyAttestation binding `node_id ↔ keys ↔ tags` (and optional DevicePostureAttestation)

### Peer discovery
Provide peer discovery and a stable view of online peers (mockable for tests).

Implementation guidance (non-binding):
- use Tailscale LocalAPI (`/localapi/v0/status`, `/localapi/v0/whois`) behind an interface

### Zone ↔ tag mapping + ACL generation
- ZoneId maps to a Tailscale tag: `tag:fcp-<suffix>`.
- Port-gating ACL generation is a defense-in-depth feature:
  - zone members can reach only zone ports (symbol_port/control_port)
  - policy engine still enforces the true integrity/confidentiality lattice

## Acceptance Criteria
- Unit tests for tag<->zone mapping.
- Mockable interface so tests don't require a real tailnet.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.17","title":"[FCP2] MeshNode: Routing, Gossip, Admission Control, Leases, Device-Aware Execution","description":"# [FCP2] MeshNode: Routing, Gossip, Admission Control, Leases, Device-Aware Execution

## Goal
Implement the core runtime node that turns the spec into behavior:
- peer discovery (via Tailscale abstraction)
- session establishment + replay policy
- symbol routing + multipath delivery
- gossip / announcements + reconciliation
- admission control + anti-amplification
- enforcement: zones/caps/taint/approvals/revocation/checkpoint/policy
- distributed coordination: leases
- connector state coordination hooks (singleton_writer)
- device-aware execution planner + delegation

## Why Users Care
MeshNode is where "mechanical security" becomes real:
- It is the enforcement boundary between untrusted inputs and privileged side effects.
- It produces DecisionReceipts and audit events, enabling `fcp explain` and incident response.
- It makes offline availability measurable (coverage) and repairable.

## Recommended Runtime Stack (2025-2026)

| Component | Crate | Version | Notes |
|-----------|-------|---------|-------|
| Async runtime | `tokio` | 1.44+ | io-uring on Linux |
| Networking | `quinn` | 0.12+ | QUIC transport |
| Gossip | `crdt-rs` / custom | - | CRDT-based state sync |
| Hash ring | `hashring` | 0.3+ | Consistent hashing |
| Timer wheel | `tokio-util` | 0.7+ | Efficient timeouts |
| Metrics | `metrics` | 0.24+ | Prometheus/OTEL |
| Tracing | `tracing` | 0.2+ | Distributed tracing |

## Normative Behaviors (Spec-Aligned)

### Default Deny Enforcement Spine
No operation executes unless it passes, in order:
1. canonical decode + schema validation
2. capability token verification (including checkpoint freshness + holder proof)
3. revocation freshness enforcement (per policy tier)
4. taint/approval enforcement (elevation/declassification)
5. zone policy ceilings + connector manifest constraints
6. (when required) execution lease acquisition/validation

Every denial MUST emit a DecisionReceipt with stable `reason_code` and evidence ObjectIds.

```rust
/// Enforcement result with full provenance
pub struct EnforcementResult {
    pub decision: Decision,
    pub reason_code: ReasonCode,
    pub evidence: Vec<ObjectId>,
    pub trace_id: TraceId,
    pub latency: Duration,
}

pub enum Decision {
    Allow { receipt: OperationReceipt },
    Deny { receipt: DecisionReceipt },
}
```

### Transport Priority + Multipath Delivery
Transport path selection is user-visible and policy-controlled.
- Priority order: Direct (LAN) → Mesh NAT traversal → DERP → Funnel
- ZoneTransportPolicy controls DERP/Funnel availability (deny-by-default for high-trust zones)
- Multipath symbol delivery MAY use multiple paths concurrently when allowed

```rust
/// Transport selection with locality scoring
pub struct TransportSelector {
    pub direct: Option<DirectPath>,
    pub mesh: Option<MeshPath>,
    pub derp: Option<DerpPath>,
    pub funnel: Option<FunnelPath>,
}

impl TransportSelector {
    pub fn best_path(&self, policy: &ZoneTransportPolicy) -> Option<&dyn Transport> {
        // Priority-ordered selection respecting policy
    }
}
```

### Admission Control + DoS Resistance (NORMATIVE)
MeshNodes MUST implement admission control for:
- per-peer inbound bytes/symbols
- failed decrypt/MAC counters
- bounded concurrent decodes + decode CPU budgets
- bounded gossip reconciliation work

```rust
/// Admission control state per peer
pub struct PeerAdmission {
    pub inbound_bytes: RateLimiter,      // Token bucket
    pub symbols_per_second: RateLimiter,
    pub failed_mac_count: AtomicU32,     // Quarantine trigger
    pub concurrent_decodes: Semaphore,
    pub quarantined_until: Option<Instant>,
}
```

Anti-amplification rule (NORMATIVE):
- do not send more than `N` symbols in response to a request unless the requester is authenticated AND presents bounded proof-of-need (e.g., missing-hint).

### Gossip Protocol (CRDT-Based)

```rust
/// Gossip message types
pub enum GossipMessage {
    /// Announce object availability (Bloom filter)
    ObjectAnnounce { filter: BloomFilter, version: u64 },
    /// Request reconciliation (XOR filter diff)
    SyncRequest { filter: XorFilter },
    /// CRDT state merge
    StateMerge { crdt: MeshStateCrdt },
}

/// CRDT for mesh state convergence
pub struct MeshStateCrdt {
    pub checkpoint_heads: LWWRegister<ZoneCheckpoint>,
    pub revocation_heads: LWWRegister<RevocationHead>,
    pub peer_liveness: GCounter,
}
```

### Control-Plane Fallback over FCPS (Spec Surface)
In degraded/offline sync scenarios, control-plane objects may be encoded to symbols and transported in FCPS frames with `FrameFlags::CONTROL_PLANE`.
MeshNode owns the semantics here (routing + reconstruction + retention enforcement).

### Execution Planner (Device-Aware)
MeshNode chooses where to execute operations based on:
- device profiles (cpu/mem/battery)
- connector availability + version requirements
- symbol locality scoring + DERP penalty
- (full profile) secret reconstruction cost

```rust
/// Device profile for execution planning
pub struct DeviceProfile {
    pub cpu_class: CpuClass,          // mobile | desktop | server
    pub memory_mb: u32,
    pub battery_powered: bool,
    pub battery_percent: Option<u8>,
    pub connector_versions: HashMap<ConnectorId, Version>,
    pub symbol_locality: f64,         // 0.0-1.0 (local availability)
}

pub enum ExecutionDecision {
    ExecuteLocal,
    DelegateToNode { node_id: NodeId, reason: String },
    WaitForSymbols { missing: Vec<SymbolId> },
    Reject { reason: ReasonCode },
}
```

## Performance Optimizations

### io_uring Integration (Linux)
```rust
#[cfg(target_os = "linux")]
pub async fn symbol_batch_read(store: &SymbolStore, ids: &[SymbolId]) -> Vec<Symbol> {
    // Use io_uring for batched reads when available
    tokio_uring::fs::read_batch(...)
}
```

### Zero-Copy Symbol Handling
```rust
/// Zero-copy symbol buffer
pub struct SymbolBuffer {
    data: bytes::Bytes,  // Reference-counted, zero-copy
    offset: usize,
}
```

## Observability

```rust
#[tracing::instrument(
    level = "info",
    skip(symbols),
    fields(
        operation = %operation_id,
        zone = %zone_id,
        peer = %peer_id,
        trace_id = %trace_context.trace_id,
    )
)]
async fn route_symbols(&self, symbols: Vec<Symbol>) -> Result<(), RoutingError> {
    metrics::counter\!("mesh.symbols.routed").increment(symbols.len() as u64);
    // ...
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- Integration tests (simulated mesh) for:
  - sessions + replay
  - routing + reconstruction
  - allow path (receipt + audit)
  - deny path (DecisionReceipt with evidence)
  - admission control rejecting floods
- Benchmarks for local invoke overhead
- CRDT gossip convergence under partition
- Device-aware execution delegation tests

## Notes
This issue is intentionally an umbrella; complex sub-behaviors are tracked as child beads (routing/multipath, admission+quarantine, gossip, execution planner, FCPS control-plane fallback).

## Goal
Validate MeshNode routing/enforcement end-to-end in tests (multi-node), including admission control, lease behavior, and policy gating, with structured logs.

## Scope
Integration tests for MeshNode routing, admission control, and policy enforcement.

## Test Categories

### Routing
- [ ] Symbol routing to correct nodes based on placement
- [ ] Control-plane message routing
- [ ] Multi-hop routing when direct path unavailable
- [ ] Routing table updates on topology changes
- [ ] Load balancing across equivalent paths

### Admission Control
- [ ] Valid requests admitted
- [ ] Requests without session rejected
- [ ] Rate-limited requests rejected with backpressure
- [ ] Quarantined nodes blocked
- [ ] Anti-amplification limits enforced

### Policy Enforcement
- [ ] Zone boundary enforcement in routing
- [ ] Capability verification before forwarding
- [ ] Revocation checks on forwarded requests
- [ ] Taint labels propagated correctly

### Gossip Integration
- [ ] Object availability propagates
- [ ] Reconciliation completes after partition
- [ ] Stale gossip is rejected
- [ ] Gossip rate limiting

### Lease Coordination
- [ ] Lease acquisition via HRW
- [ ] Lease renewal works
- [ ] Lease transfer on node failure
- [ ] Lease conflicts detected

## Golden Vectors
Location: `tests/vectors/mesh_node/`
- `routing_table.json` - Expected routing decisions
- `admission_scenarios.json` - Allow/deny test cases
- `gossip_messages.cbor` - Gossip message examples

## Test Infrastructure
- Deterministic mesh harness (3+ nodes)
- Network fault injection
- Clock control for timeouts
- Structured log collection

## Logging Requirements
All tests MUST emit structured JSON:
```json
{
  "timestamp": "RFC3339",
  "test_name": "test_symbol_routing",
  "module": "fcp-mesh",
  "phase": "execute",

### For Admission Tests

### For Gossip Tests

  "object_id": "...",
  "propagation_time_ms": 50,
  "nodes_reached": 3
}
```

## CI Integration
```bash
cargo test -p fcp-mesh --test mesh_integration -- --nocapture --test-threads=1
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- All routing scenarios tested
- Admission control is complete
- Gossip converges correctly
- Structured logs produced

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.17.2","title":"[FCP2-MESH] Transport Priority + Multipath Delivery Engine","description":"# [FCP2-MESH] Transport Priority + Multipath Delivery Engine

## Goal
Implement spec-aligned **path selection** and **multipath symbol delivery**.

## Requirements
- Transport priority order (default):
  1. Tailscale Direct (same LAN)
  2. Tailscale Mesh (NAT traversal)
  3. Tailscale DERP Relay (policy-controlled per zone)
  4. Tailscale Funnel/public ingress (policy-controlled; low-trust zones only by default)

- ZoneTransportPolicy MUST be enforced:
  - high-trust zones can forbid DERP/Funnel
  - low-trust zones can allow Funnel with explicit constraints

- Multipath symbol delivery:
  - when multiple paths are available and allowed by policy, MeshNode can send/receive symbols on multiple paths concurrently
  - load balancing strategy must be deterministic (so behavior is testable and reproducible)

## Deliverables
- A routing/path-selection module that:
  - ranks candidate paths based on priority + penalties (e.g., DERP penalty)
  - emits structured logs of chosen path and policy decision
- Multipath sender/receiver plumbing:
  - consistent session establishment per peer
  - bounded concurrency and backpressure

## Tests
- Deterministic tests for path ranking given a fixed peer topology.
- Policy tests: DERP forbidden in z:owner-like zones yields denial reason codes.

## Acceptance Criteria
- In the E2E harness, symbol transfer works over each supported path type.
- Logs clearly show which path was used and why.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.17.3","title":"[FCP2-MESH] Admission Control + Anti-Amplification + Quarantine Enforcement","description":"# [FCP2-MESH] Admission Control + Anti-Amplification + Quarantine Enforcement

## Goal
Implement the **DoS resistance** and **admission-control** requirements for the mesh layer.

This is non-negotiable: without bounded budgets, the mesh becomes the largest attack surface.

## Normative requirements (spec-aligned)
MeshNodes MUST implement admission control for:
- per-peer inbound bytes/symbols
- failed decrypt/MAC counters
- bounded concurrent decodes
- bounded gossip reconciliation work

### PeerBudget (defaults are policy-tunable)
- `max_bytes_per_min` (default 64MB/min)
- `max_symbols_per_min` (default 200k/min)
- `max_failed_auth_per_min` (default 100/min)
- `max_inflight_decodes` (default 32)
- `max_decode_cpu_ms_per_min` (default 5000ms/min)

### Anti-amplification rule (NORMATIVE)
MeshNodes MUST NOT send more than `N` symbols in response to a request unless the requester:
1. is authenticated (session MAC or node signature), AND
2. presents bounded proof-of-need (e.g., DecodeStatus.missing_hint) or comparable evidence.

### Quarantine enforcement (owned jointly with Stores)
- Unknown/unreferenced ObjectIds MUST be quarantined by default (bounded TTL + quotas).
- Quarantined objects MUST NOT be inserted into global gossip state.
- Promotion rules are strict (reachability from ZoneCheckpoint / authenticated bounded request / explicit pin) and MUST require schema validation.

## Deliverables
- A single admission-control module used consistently by:
  - FCPS datagram handling
  - symbol_request handling
  - gossip reconciliation
  - decode/reconstruction scheduling
- Explicit reason codes + structured logs for rejections.

## Tests
- Property tests: budgets cannot go negative; counters are monotonic and bounded.
- Adversarial tests:
  - flood of invalid MACs triggers peer ban/throttle
  - decode CPU DoS corpus stays within time bounds
  - quarantine quotas enforce eviction

## Acceptance Criteria
- E2E harness can simulate malicious peers and show bounded impact.
- Logs clearly explain admission-control denials.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.17.4","title":"[FCP2-MESH] SymbolRequest + Targeted Repair (DecodeStatus/SymbolAck)","description":"# [FCP2-MESH] SymbolRequest + Targeted Repair (DecodeStatus/SymbolAck)

## Goal
Make symbol transfer efficient and DoS-resistant by implementing:
- bounded SymbolRequest handling
- flow-control feedback via DecodeStatus
- stop conditions via SymbolAck
- targeted repair (send missing symbols, not floods)

## Normative requirements (spec-aligned)
### SymbolRequest bounding
- A requester MUST bound requests (max_symbols and/or missing-hint proof-of-need).
- A responder MUST NOT send more than `max_symbols` symbols in response.
- Unauthenticated requests MUST be capped tighter (default max_symbols_unauthenticated = 32) unless zone policy allows.
- Accounting rule: SymbolRequest processing counts against PeerBudget (bytes + CPU + inflight decodes).

### DecodeStatus (control-plane object)
- Receiver reports how many unique symbols received vs required, optionally with a compact missing-hint.
- Used for targeted repair and anti-amplification compliance.

### SymbolAck (control-plane object)
- Receiver can tell sender to stop: object reconstructed.

## Deliverables
- Canonical object schemas for DecodeStatus + SymbolAck (retention: Ephemeral).
- MeshNode handlers that:
  - validate bounds and enforce admission control
  - use missing-hint when present for targeted repair
  - stop sending on SymbolAck
  - emit structured logs/metrics for repair actions

## Tests
- Property tests: bounded requests never trigger unbounded sends.
- Adversarial tests: spoofed SymbolRequests can’t amplify traffic.
- Integration tests in E2E harness: reconstruction converges faster with targeted repair.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Multi-node simulation shows stable convergence under loss without budget blowups.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-h1wi","title":"TEST: Connector Performance Benchmark Suite","description":"# TEST: Connector Performance Benchmark Suite (FCP2)


## Goal
Establish a benchmark suite to measure and guard connector performance budgets (latency, memory, allocations) with regression tracking.

## Overview
Performance benchmarks ensuring the platform meets the README/spec targets.

Benchmarks must be:
- repeatable
- machine-readable
- diagnostic (logs + environment metadata)

## Benchmarks (README-aligned)
### `fcp bench connector-activate`
- cold start time (p50/p99)
- warm start time (optional)

### `fcp bench invoke-local`
- end-to-end invoke latency when caller/executor are the same node

### `fcp bench invoke-mesh --path=direct|derp`
- end-to-end invoke latency across the mesh for:
  - direct/LAN
  - DERP

### `fcp bench raptorq --size=1mb`
- encode/decode throughput and wall time
- memory high-water mark if available

### `fcp bench secrets --k=3 --n=5`
- secret reconstruction wall time + overhead

### Microbenches (hot primitives)
- canonical CBOR encode/decode
- ObjectId derivation
- CapabilityToken verify
- FCPS frame parse + session MAC verify

## Output
- JSON results with:
  - benchmark name + parameters
  - p50/p90/p99
  - environment (os/arch/cpu)
  - versions + commit hash
- Regression detection policy:
  - start with “report-only”
  - later enforce thresholds once baselines stabilize

## Logging Requirements
Per `flywheel_connectors-1n78.35`, logs must include:
- bench name/params
- sample counts
- any detected outliers

## Acceptance Criteria
- Suite produces stable JSON shapes suitable for snapshot testing.
- Results are reproducible enough to catch real regressions (not noise).

-{"id":"flywheel_connectors-h32","title":"STANDARD: Connector Testing Requirements and Quality Gates","description":"# STANDARD: Connector Testing Requirements and Quality Gates

## Goal
Define mandatory connector testing quality gates (mock-only unit tests + E2E compliance runs) so every connector is mechanically verifiable.

## Overview
Defines mandatory testing requirements and quality gates that ALL FCP connectors must pass before release.

This standard is connector-focused and builds on the platform-wide test/logging standard `flywheel_connectors-1n78.35`.

## Recommended Testing Stack (2025-2026)

| Category | Tool | Version | Notes |
|----------|------|---------|-------|
| Unit framework | `cargo test` | built-in | Standard Rust testing |
| Snapshot | `insta` | 1.40+ | JSON + terminal snapshots |
| Property | `proptest` | 0.11+ | Invariant/fuzzy testing |
| Mutation | `cargo-mutants` | 25.1+ | Mutation coverage |
| Fuzzing | `cargo-fuzz` | 0.12+ | Fuzz targets for parsing |
| HTTP mock | `wiremock` | 0.6+ | Async HTTP mocking |
| Assertions | `assert2` | 0.3+ | Better error messages |
| Coverage | `cargo-llvm-cov` | 0.6+ | LLVM-based coverage |
| Security | `cargo-audit` | 0.21+ | Dependency audit |
| Benchmarks | `criterion` | 0.6+ | Statistical benchmarks |

## Unit Test Requirements

### Coverage Thresholds
- Overall: >80% line coverage
- Critical paths: >95% coverage
- Security code: >98% coverage
- **Mutation score**: >70% (cargo-mutants)

### Required Test Categories
- [ ] All connector standard methods (`--manifest`, handshake/describe/introspect/capabilities/configure/invoke/health/shutdown)
- [ ] Configuration validation (valid/invalid)
- [ ] Handshake success/failure scenarios
- [ ] Capability token validation
- [ ] Each operation (success, common errors, edge cases)
- [ ] Rate limit handling
- [ ] Timeout behavior
- [ ] Error recovery
- [ ] **Property tests**: Random inputs for all string/struct parsing

### Mocking Requirements
- NEVER make real API calls in unit tests
- Use wiremock or similar for HTTP mocking
- Mock external service responses
- Test error injection (network, auth, rate limit)

### Property Testing Patterns
```rust
proptest\! {
    #[test]
    fn parse_config_doesnt_panic(input in ".*") {
        // Should never panic, only return Ok/Err
        let _ = ConnectorConfig::parse(&input);
    }
    
    #[test]
    fn roundtrip_serialization(config in arb_connector_config()) {
        let serialized = serde_json::to_string(&config)?;
        let deserialized: ConnectorConfig = serde_json::from_str(&serialized)?;
        prop_assert_eq\!(config, deserialized);
    }
}
```

## Fuzz Testing Requirements (Security-Critical)

### Required Fuzz Targets
Every connector MUST have fuzz targets for:
- [ ] Configuration parsing
- [ ] Protocol message parsing
- [ ] Credential/token handling
- [ ] Event payload parsing

### Fuzz Target Template
```rust
#\![no_main]
use libfuzzer_sys::fuzz_target;

fuzz_target\!(|data: &[u8]| {
    // Should never panic, only return valid error
    let _ = connector::parse_message(data);
});
```

### CI Fuzz Integration
```yaml
- name: Fuzz for 60s per target
  run: cargo +nightly fuzz run --release --sanitizer=address -- -max_total_time=60
```

## Integration Test Requirements
- [ ] Real handshake flow (with test credentials) OR fully deterministic harness
- [ ] Basic operation execution
- [ ] Event subscription lifecycle
- [ ] Reconnection handling

## E2E Test Requirements (Mechanical Compliance)
Every connector MUST pass the platform E2E/compliance harness:
- [ ] Mechanical compliance runner (static + dynamic)
- [ ] Zone binding enforcement
- [ ] Default deny (no cap token → denial + DecisionReceipt)
- [ ] No secret leakage in logs
- [ ] Sandbox/network constraints enforced
- [ ] **Tool descriptor validation** (SEP-1382 compliance)

## Security Testing Requirements

### cargo-audit Gate
```yaml
- name: Security audit
  run: cargo audit --deny warnings --ignore RUSTSEC-XXXX
```

### Secret Leakage Detection
```rust
#[test]
fn no_secrets_in_logs() {
    let logs = capture_connector_logs();
    assert\!(\!logs.contains("api_key="));
    assert\!(\!logs.contains("Authorization:"));
    assert\!(\!logs.contains("Bearer "));
}
```

### Zeroize Verification
```rust
#[test]
fn credentials_are_zeroized_on_drop() {
    let cred = Credential::new("secret");
    let ptr = cred.as_ptr();
    drop(cred);
    // Memory should be zeroed
    unsafe { assert\!(std::slice::from_raw_parts(ptr, 6).iter().all(|&b| b == 0)); }
}
```

## Quality Gates (CI/CD)
1. `cargo check --all-targets` - No errors
2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` - No warnings
3. `cargo fmt --check` - Properly formatted
4. `cargo test` - All tests pass
5. `cargo test --release` - Release tests pass
6. Coverage threshold met (>80%)
7. `cargo audit --deny warnings` - Security scan clean
8. `cargo mutants --timeout-multiplier=2` - Mutation score >70%
9. Fuzz targets run without crash

## Connector Plan Template

+{"id":"flywheel_connectors-h32","title":"STANDARD: Connector Testing Requirements and Quality Gates","description":"# STANDARD: Connector Testing Requirements and Quality Gates

## Goal
Define mandatory connector testing quality gates (mock-only unit tests + E2E compliance runs) so every connector is mechanically verifiable.

## Overview
Defines mandatory testing requirements and quality gates that ALL FCP connectors must pass before release.

This standard is connector-focused and builds on the platform-wide test/logging standard `flywheel_connectors-1n78.35`.

## Recommended Testing Stack (2025-2026)

| Category | Tool | Version | Notes |
|----------|------|---------|-------|
| Unit framework | `cargo test` | built-in | Standard Rust testing |
| Snapshot | `insta` | 1.40+ | JSON + terminal snapshots |
| Property | `proptest` | 0.11+ | Invariant/fuzzy testing |
| Mutation | `cargo-mutants` | 25.1+ | Mutation coverage |
| Fuzzing | `cargo-fuzz` | 0.12+ | Fuzz targets for parsing |
| HTTP mock | `wiremock` | 0.6+ | Async HTTP mocking |
| Assertions | `assert2` | 0.3+ | Better error messages |
| Coverage | `cargo-llvm-cov` | 0.6+ | LLVM-based coverage |
| Security | `cargo-audit` | 0.21+ | Dependency audit |
| Benchmarks | `criterion` | 0.6+ | Statistical benchmarks |

## Unit Test Requirements

### Coverage Thresholds
- Overall: >80% line coverage
- Critical paths: >95% coverage
- Security code: >98% coverage
- **Mutation score**: >70% (cargo-mutants)

### Required Test Categories
- [ ] All connector standard methods (`--manifest`, handshake/describe/introspect/capabilities/configure/invoke/health/shutdown)
- [ ] Configuration validation (valid/invalid)
- [ ] Handshake success/failure scenarios
- [ ] Capability token validation
- [ ] Each operation (success, common errors, edge cases)
- [ ] Rate limit handling
- [ ] Timeout behavior
- [ ] Error recovery
- [ ] **Property tests**: Random inputs for all string/struct parsing

### Mocking Requirements
- NEVER make real API calls in unit tests
- Use wiremock or similar for HTTP mocking
- Mock external service responses
- Test error injection (network, auth, rate limit)

### Property Testing Patterns
```rust
proptest\! {
    #[test]
    fn parse_config_doesnt_panic(input in ".*") {
        // Should never panic, only return Ok/Err
        let _ = ConnectorConfig::parse(&input);
    }
    
    #[test]
    fn roundtrip_serialization(config in arb_connector_config()) {
        let serialized = serde_json::to_string(&config)?;
        let deserialized: ConnectorConfig = serde_json::from_str(&serialized)?;
        prop_assert_eq\!(config, deserialized);
    }
}
```

## Fuzz Testing Requirements (Security-Critical)

### Required Fuzz Targets
Every connector MUST have fuzz targets for:
- [ ] Configuration parsing
- [ ] Protocol message parsing
- [ ] Credential/token handling
- [ ] Event payload parsing

### Fuzz Target Template
```rust
#\![no_main]
use libfuzzer_sys::fuzz_target;

fuzz_target\!(|data: &[u8]| {
    // Should never panic, only return valid error
    let _ = connector::parse_message(data);
});
```

### CI Fuzz Integration
```yaml
- name: Fuzz for 60s per target
  run: cargo +nightly fuzz run --release --sanitizer=address -- -max_total_time=60
```

## Integration Test Requirements
- [ ] Real handshake flow (with test credentials) OR fully deterministic harness
- [ ] Basic operation execution
- [ ] Event subscription lifecycle
- [ ] Reconnection handling

## E2E Test Requirements (Mechanical Compliance)
Every connector MUST pass the platform E2E/compliance harness:
- [ ] Mechanical compliance runner (static + dynamic)
- [ ] Zone binding enforcement
- [ ] Default deny (no cap token → denial + DecisionReceipt)
- [ ] No secret leakage in logs
- [ ] Sandbox/network constraints enforced
- [ ] **Tool descriptor validation** (SEP-1382 compliance)

## Security Testing Requirements

### cargo-audit Gate
```yaml
- name: Security audit
  run: cargo audit --deny warnings --ignore RUSTSEC-XXXX
```

### Secret Leakage Detection
```rust
#[test]
fn no_secrets_in_logs() {
    let logs = capture_connector_logs();
    assert\!(\!logs.contains("api_key="));
    assert\!(\!logs.contains("Authorization:"));
    assert\!(\!logs.contains("Bearer "));
}
```

### Zeroize Verification
```rust
#[test]
fn credentials_are_zeroized_on_drop() {
    let cred = Credential::new("secret");
    let ptr = cred.as_ptr();
    drop(cred);
    // Memory should be zeroed
    unsafe { assert\!(std::slice::from_raw_parts(ptr, 6).iter().all(|&b| b == 0)); }
}
```

## Quality Gates (CI/CD)
1. `cargo check --all-targets` - No errors
2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` - No warnings
3. `cargo fmt --check` - Properly formatted
4. `cargo test` - All tests pass
5. `cargo test --release` - Release tests pass
6. Coverage threshold met (>80%)
7. `cargo audit --deny warnings` - Security scan clean
8. `cargo mutants --timeout-multiplier=2` - Mutation score >70%
9. Fuzz targets run without crash

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-hc2","title":"fcp.cron: Scheduled Task Runner","description":"# Scheduled Task Runner Meta-Connector

**Force Multiplier:** Time-based automation for FCP zones.

## Overview

Many automation workflows require scheduled execution: daily reports, periodic syncs, cleanup jobs, reminder systems. This meta-connector provides a robust scheduling infrastructure that triggers FCP actions on time-based schedules.

## Core Components

### 1. Cron Expression Scheduling
- Standard cron syntax (5-field and 6-field)
- Extended syntax with seconds precision
- Timezone-aware scheduling
- Named schedules for common patterns
- Cron expression validation and preview

```
# Standard patterns
@hourly    = 0 * * * *
@daily     = 0 0 * * *
@weekly    = 0 0 * * 0
@monthly   = 0 0 1 * *

# Custom patterns
*/15 * * * *     = Every 15 minutes
0 9 * * 1-5      = 9 AM on weekdays
0 0 1,15 * *     = 1st and 15th of month
```

### 2. One-Time Scheduled Tasks
- Schedule task for specific datetime
- Support for relative scheduling ("in 30 minutes")
- Cancellation before execution
- Rescheduling capability
- Batch scheduling (multiple one-time tasks)

### 3. Interval-Based Triggers
- Fixed interval execution (every N minutes/hours/days)
- Sliding window intervals
- Jitter support for load distribution
- Minimum interval enforcement
- Pause/resume capability

### 4. Task History and Logs
- Complete execution history
- Stdout/stderr capture
- Execution duration tracking
- Success/failure status
- Searchable log storage
- Log retention policies

### 5. Failure Alerting
- Configurable alert channels (email, Slack, PagerDuty)
- Alert on:
  - Task failure
  - Task timeout
  - Consecutive failures
  - Missed schedules
- Alert suppression/snooze
- Escalation policies

## Configuration Example

```toml
[[schedules]]
name = "daily-backup"
cron = "0 2 * * *"
timezone = "America/New_York"
action = "zone.backup.run_backup"
params = { full = true }
timeout = "1h"
alert_on_failure = true

[[schedules]]
name = "hourly-sync"
interval = "1h"
jitter = "5m"
action = "zone.sync.pull_updates"
retry_on_failure = 3

[[one_time]]
name = "migration-task"
run_at = "2024-02-01T03:00:00Z"
action = "zone.db.run_migration"
params = { version = "v2.0" }
```

## Task State Machine

```
PENDING → RUNNING → COMPLETED
              ↓
           FAILED → RETRYING → COMPLETED/FAILED
              ↓
         DEAD (max retries exceeded)
```

## Dashboard Features

- Calendar view of scheduled tasks
- Next N upcoming executions
- Recent execution history
- Live execution monitoring
- Manual trigger capability
- Bulk operations (pause all, resume all)

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Sub-second scheduling precision
- [ ] Support 1000+ concurrent schedules
- [ ] Missed task detection and alerting
- [ ] Distributed execution (no single point of failure)
- [ ] Complete audit trail
- [ ] Hot-reload of schedule configurations

## Dependencies

- Persistent storage for schedules and history
- Time synchronization (NTP)
- Alert channel integrations
- Zone action invocation system

## Considerations

- Handle daylight saving time transitions
- Cluster-safe (only one instance executes)
- Graceful shutdown (complete running tasks)
- Clock skew tolerance
- Long-running task support","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","assignee":"","created_at":"2026-01-11T15:23:20.104073834Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-11T15:53:35.225906099Z","closed_at":"2026-01-11T15:53:35.225906099Z","close_reason":"Duplicate of flywheel_connectors-hiu (fcp.cron: Scheduled Task Runner Connector)","compaction_level":0}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-heeq","title":"Threshold Secrets (Shamir Secret Sharing)","description":"## Overview

Implement cryptographic threshold secret sharing using Shamir's Secret Sharing Scheme (SSS) over GF(2^8) as mandated by V2 spec §17.3. This is a DISTINCT system from RaptorQ symbol distribution - secrets use real cryptographic secret sharing because RaptorQ symbols can leak structure.

## Background & Justification

**Why This Matters:**
The V2 spec explicitly states: "Secrets use real cryptographic secret sharing (Shamir), not just RaptorQ symbols. RaptorQ symbols are NOT a secret sharing scheme—a single symbol can leak structure."

Key differences from RaptorQ:
- RaptorQ symbols can leak structure (not semantically secure)
- Single RaptorQ symbol may reveal partial information
- Shamir shares reveal NOTHING until k shares collected
- Wrapped shares ensure a node cannot decrypt other nodes' shares

**Design Philosophy:**
"Secrets are never complete anywhere" - the secret is only reconstructed ephemerally during use and immediately zeroized. No single device ever stores the complete secret.

## Normative Types (V2 Spec §17.3)

### SecretObject
```rust
pub struct SecretObject {
    pub header: ObjectHeader,
    pub secret_id: SecretId,        // Unique identifier
    pub zone_id: ZoneId,            // Zone binding
    pub k: u8,                      // Threshold (need k shares)
    pub n: u8,                      // Total shares distributed
    pub scheme: SecretSharingScheme,
    pub wrapped_shares: HashMap<TailscaleNodeId, Vec<u8>>,
    pub rotation: SecretRotationPolicy,
}
```

### SecretSharingScheme
```rust
pub enum SecretSharingScheme {
    ShamirGf256,  // Shamir's Secret Sharing over GF(2^8)
}
```

### SecretRotationPolicy
```rust
pub struct SecretRotationPolicy {
    pub rotate_after_secs: u64,    // Auto-rotate interval
    pub overlap_secs: u64,         // Both old and new valid
}
```

### SecretAccessToken
```rust
pub struct SecretAccessToken {
    pub jti: Uuid,                  // Unique token ID
    pub secret_id: SecretId,        // Which secret
    pub purpose: String,            // For audit trail
    pub requested_by: PrincipalId,  // Requester identity
    pub iat: u64,                   // Issued at
    pub exp: u64,                   // Expires at (short-lived!)
    pub signature: Signature,       // Approver signature
}
```

## Implementation Requirements

### 1. Shamir Secret Sharing Implementation
- Implement Shamir SSS over GF(2^8) with constant-time operations
- Support configurable k-of-n thresholds
- Each share MUST be wrapped (encrypted) so nodes cannot decrypt other nodes' shares
- Use per-node Tailscale keys for share wrapping

### 2. SecretObject Management
- Create/store secrets with proper threshold configuration
- Distribute wrapped shares to mesh peers
- Track share locations for reconstruction
- Implement zone-scoped access control

### 3. Secret Reconstruction (Ephemeral)
```rust
impl SecretObject {
    pub async fn use_secret<F, R>(&self, access_token: &SecretAccessToken, f: F) -> Result<R>
    where F: FnOnce(&[u8]) -> R
    {
        // 1. Verify access token
        access_token.verify()?;
        
        // 2. Create audit event BEFORE reconstruction
        self.audit_secret_access(access_token).await?;
        
        // 3. Collect k wrapped shares from peers
        let shares = self.collect_k_wrapped_shares(access_token).await?;
        
        // 4. Reconstruct using Shamir
        let secret = reconstruct_shamir_secure(&shares)?;
        
        // 5. Execute closure with ephemeral secret
        let result = f(&secret);
        
        // 6. Zeroize IMMEDIATELY after use
        secure_zero(secret);
        
        Ok(result)
    }
}
```

### 4. SecretAccessToken Workflow
- Token issuance requires owner or delegated approver signature
- Tokens MUST be short-lived (minutes, not hours)
- Purpose field required for audit trail
- Verify before any secret operation

### 5. Secret Rotation
- Implement automatic rotation based on SecretRotationPolicy
- Support overlap period where both old and new secrets valid
- Coordinate rotation across mesh peers
- Maintain audit trail of rotation events

### 6. Integration with Credentials
The CredentialObject (§10.4) references secrets via SecretId:
- CredentialObject.secret_ref: Option<SecretId>
- Secret materialization requires SecretAccessToken
- Audit every secret access for credential injection

## Security Considerations

1. **Constant-time operations**: All Shamir math MUST be constant-time to prevent timing attacks
2. **Zeroization**: Use zeroize crate; secrets never persist in memory longer than closure execution
3. **Share isolation**: Wrapped shares use per-node keys; compromise of one node doesn't leak other shares
4. **Audit trail**: Every secret access creates AuditEvent BEFORE reconstruction
5. **Token validation**: SecretAccessToken signature and expiry verified before any operation

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Shamir GF(2^8) implementation with constant-time polynomial evaluation
- [ ] SecretObject with k-of-n threshold configuration
- [ ] Per-node share wrapping using Tailscale node keys
- [ ] SecretAccessToken creation and validation
- [ ] Ephemeral reconstruction with immediate zeroization
- [ ] SecretRotationPolicy with overlap period support
- [ ] Integration with CredentialObject for secret-backed credentials
- [ ] Comprehensive audit events for all secret operations
- [ ] Unit tests for Shamir math, threshold edge cases, rotation
- [ ] Integration tests for multi-node secret reconstruction

## Dependencies

This feature depends on:
- Core Types (ObjectHeader, ZoneId, etc.)
- Zone Architecture (zone-scoped access)
- Capability System (for SecretAccessToken authorization)
- MeshNode (for peer share collection)
- Observability (for audit events)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","assignee":"","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-15T09:41:27.758361051Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-15T09:47:20.445243106Z","closed_at":"2026-01-15T09:47:20.445243106Z","close_reason":"Duplicate of 6o25.1 (enhanced with detailed V2 spec content)","compaction_level":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-heeq","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.17","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-15T09:42:48.351360458Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-heeq","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.28","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-15T09:42:49.218849666Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-heeq","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-15T09:41:52.710979314Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-lszk","title":"[FCP2] Connector Library: V2-Compliant Connectors","description":"# [FCP2] Connector Library: V2-Compliant Connectors

## Goal
Build a library of production connectors that **fully comply** with the FCP V2 platform:
- single-zone binding
- default deny via capabilities
- revocation freshness enforcement
- correct receipts and audit events
- sandbox + network constraints
- no secrets on disk; no secret logging

This epic intentionally assumes the **core platform** exists first.

## Entry criteria (what must exist before connectors are real)
Connectors should not begin until these are available:
- `[FCP2] Connector SDK (Rust)`
- `[FCP2] Connector Manifest (TOML)`
- `[FCP2] Policy Engine` (DecisionReceipts + reason codes)
- `[FCP2] Sandbox + Egress Proxy`
- `[FCP2] MeshNode` (enforcement boundary)
- `[FCP2] Registry + Supply Chain` (install/verify)
- `[FCP2] Conformance` (golden vectors + fuzz + compliance runner)

## Definition of Done
- Each connector has:
  - a manifest with explicit capabilities + sandbox profile
  - a conformance test suite (unit + integration with mocks)

- Mechanical compliance checking is part of the conformance suite.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-lszk.1","title":"fcp.telegram - Telegram Connector (V2)","description":"# fcp.telegram - Telegram Connector (FCP2)

## Goal
Provide a **fully FCP2-compliant** Telegram Bot integration for secure automation in high-risk chat surfaces.

This connector must be:
- **Single-zone bound** for its lifetime (one instance ↔ one `ZoneId`).
- **Default-deny**: every external action requires explicit CapabilityTokens.
- **Mechanically sandboxed**: NetworkConstraints + OS/WASI sandbox enforced by the host.
- **Secrets-safe**: bot tokens never touch disk; never appear in logs.

## Manifest archetypes (closed set)
- `bidirectional` (send + receive)
- `streaming` (message/update events)
- `operational` (request/response actions)

> Note: polling/webhooks are *interaction patterns*, not manifest archetype values.

## Threat model (why this connector is special)
Telegram content is an adversarial prompt surface.

Hard requirements:
- All inbound Telegram content is tainted as **external input** by default.
- The connector must emit provenance/taint metadata so policy can enforce:
  - deny-by-default cross-zone data movement
  - declassification only with explicit ApprovalToken scope
  - sanitizer receipts for any “cleaning” step

## Zone model (mechanical)

<!-- fcp2-single-zone-note -->
**FCP2 single-zone binding note:** A connector instance binds to exactly one `ZoneId` for its entire lifetime.
The *"zones"* described in this section are a **recommended deployment pattern** (run separate connector instances in separate zones with different capability grants/policies) and/or conceptual risk tiers.
The connector MUST NOT multiplex multiple zones internally.

Each connector instance binds to exactly one zone. Recommended zone naming conventions (policy-owned, not connector-owned):
- `z:community:telegram:<group_or_guild>` (semi-trusted communities)
- `z:public:telegram:<channel>` (public channels)
- `z:owner:telegram:<dm>` (explicitly opted-in DMs)

## External surface
### NetworkConstraints (MUST)
- Allow egress only to Telegram Bot API endpoints:
  - `api.telegram.org:443`
- Deny IP literals by default; require hostname canonicalization.
- No raw sockets in strict/moderate sandboxes; all egress goes through the Network Guard.

### Auth / credential injection
- Bot token provided via FCP2 `CredentialObject`/`CredentialId` injection (egress proxy preferred).
- Never persist token (or derived bearer headers) to disk.

## Capability model (illustrative, final IDs defined in manifest bead)
**NORMATIVE constraint:** capability IDs must not encode hosts/ports.

Capability families:
- `telegram.messages.read` (receive/poll updates; creates EventEnvelopes)
- `telegram.messages.send` (side-effecting send)
- `telegram.media.read` (download attachments)
- `telegram.media.send` (upload media)
- `telegram.chats.read` (list/get chat metadata)
- `telegram.members.read` (chat member info; treat as PII)
- `telegram.webhooks.manage` (Dangerous; requires explicit policy + ApprovalToken `Execution` scope)

## Operations & event topics (initial slice)
Keep the first slice tight and correct; expand after conformance is stable.

### Operations
- `telegram.send_message` (side-effect; requires `telegram.messages.send`)
- `telegram.send_media` (side-effect; requires `telegram.media.send`)
- `telegram.get_chat` / `telegram.list_chats` (read-only; requires `telegram.chats.read`)
- `telegram.get_updates` (streaming source; requires `telegram.messages.read`)
- `telegram.download_file` (read-only; requires `telegram.media.read`)

### Event topics
- `telegram.message.new`
- `telegram.message.edited`
- `telegram.chat.member_joined`
- `telegram.chat.member_left`

## State model
Telegram polling is inherently stateful.

- Use **singleton-writer** state with a lease (to avoid two instances consuming the same update stream).
- Persist cursor/offset in a state object (e.g., last `update_id` + replay/ack cursor).
- Side-effect idempotency:
  - Prefer **fail-closed** on uncertain outcomes (timeout after request sent) to avoid duplicate sends.
  - When the remote API provides a definitive message id response, bind it to an OperationReceipt.

## Rate limiting & timeouts
- Enforce Telegram Bot API limits mechanically (connector + platform). No “best effort”.
- All network calls must be bounded by timeouts; no unbounded long-polls outside allowed sandbox policy.

## Observability & audit
- Structured logs with redaction (no token, no raw message bodies by default).
- Emit AuditEvents for:
  - credential injection usage
  - side-effecting sends
  - webhook configuration changes
  - repeated denials / policy violations

## Test plan (must be mechanical)
- **Unit tests**: pure logic (parsers, schema validation, state machine) + wiremock HTTP client.
- **Integration tests**: deterministic fake Bot API server producing scripted updates.
- **E2E compliance**: run through the Mechanical Connector Compliance Runner:
  - `--manifest` correctness (archetypes, NetworkConstraints, capability map)
  - default deny (no token → denial + DecisionReceipt)
  - sandbox/network enforcement (deny any non-telegram host)
  - taint/provenance labeling on inbound events
  - structured JSON logs with correlation IDs

## Acceptance Criteria
- Passes connector compliance runner (static + dynamic).
- Passes integration tests without real Telegram calls.
- Meets cold-start and steady-state budgets (target: <50ms cold start; <10MB idle).

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-lszk.1.2","title":"fcp.telegram: Provisioning Automation (Bot setup)","description":"# fcp.telegram: Provisioning Automation (Bot setup)

## Goal
Make Telegram setup **automatic and low-friction** while remaining security-first and zone-scoped.

## What must be automated
- Validate that a provided bot token is syntactically valid and works (via a safe read-only call).
- Surface minimal user prompts (only what cannot be inferred).

## Provisioning interface
Use the platform AutomationRecipe/Provisioning interface:
- “I need a bot token” is modeled as a `CredentialId` requirement.
- The recipe verifies token correctness without leaking it.

## Optional: webhook mode (later)
If/when we support Telegram webhooks:
- Connector must NOT expose an inbound port directly.
- `fcp-host` owns the public endpoint; it forwards verified webhook payloads over FCPC.
- Enabling/disabling webhooks is a **Dangerous** action:
  - requires `telegram.webhooks.manage`
  - requires ApprovalToken `Execution` scope
  - emits AuditEvents

## UX requirements
- Clear errors for common mistakes:
  - wrong token
  - bot blocked by chat
  - insufficient permissions
- A “doctor” check path that validates:
  - token valid
  - network constraints allow Telegram
  - rate-limit config sane

## Acceptance Criteria
- Provisioning flow can bring a new Telegram bot online with minimal steps.
- All verification is mockable for unit tests.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-lszk.1.3","title":"fcp.telegram: Polling + Event Streaming (getUpdates)","description":"# fcp.telegram: Polling + Event Streaming (getUpdates)

## Goal
Implement Telegram inbound updates as an FCP2 streaming source:
- consume updates from Telegram Bot API (`getUpdates` long-poll)
- emit FCP2 `EventEnvelope`s with correct topics, taints, and cursors

## Core requirements
### Singleton-writer
- The poll loop MUST be protected by a singleton-writer lease.
- Non-lease-holder instances must fail closed (no polling).

### Cursor model
- Persist `update_id` cursor in connector state.
- Ensure restart resumes without re-emitting old updates.

### Event emission
- Map Telegram update types → event topics.
- All inbound content must be tainted as external input.
- Attach sufficient provenance (chat id, message id, update id) without leaking secrets.

### Backpressure + bounds
- Bound poll concurrency and queue sizes.
- No unbounded memory growth on high-volume chats.

### Timeouts
- Long poll timeout is bounded and policy-controlled.
- Cancellation/shutdown must be prompt.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Deterministic integration tests can script an update stream and observe emitted EventEnvelopes.
- Replay/ack behavior is consistent with the connector SDK’s streaming contract.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-lszk.1.4","title":"fcp.telegram: Send + Media Operations (receipts, fail-closed)","description":"# fcp.telegram: Send + Media Operations (receipts, fail-closed)

## Goal
Implement the side-effecting operation surface for Telegram in a way that is:
- capability-gated
- auditable
- safe under retries and partial failures

## Operations (initial)
- `telegram.send_message`
- `telegram.send_media`

## Exactly-once / retry safety
Telegram does not provide robust server-side idempotency keys.

Design requirement:
- If the connector cannot prove whether a send succeeded (e.g., timeout after request was sent), it MUST **fail closed** to avoid accidental duplicate sends.
- When a send succeeds and returns a definitive `message_id`, bind that to an OperationReceipt.

## Audit requirements
- Emit AuditEvents for each side-effecting send (redacting message bodies by default).
- Include correlation IDs that allow tracing user intent → invoke → receipt.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Conformance runner can validate default-deny and that allowed calls produce receipts.
- Integration tests cover timeout/partial failure behavior.

+## 9. Connector-Specific Testing Requirements (NORMATIVE)
+
+> **Bead Reference**: `flywheel_connectors-h32`
+
+These requirements apply to **every connector** in addition to the platform-wide rules above.
+
+### 9.1 Required Connector Test Categories
+
+Each connector MUST include unit/integration tests for:
+- **Standard methods**: `--manifest`, `configure`, `handshake`, `health`, `introspect`,
+  `invoke`, `subscribe`/`unsubscribe` (if supported), `shutdown`.
+- **Configuration validation**: valid/invalid config payloads, missing fields, type errors.
+- **Capability token validation**: expired tokens, wrong zone, missing operation grant.
+- **Operation coverage**: each operation's success path + common failure paths + edge cases.
+- **Rate limiting + timeouts**: enforce limits and retry/backoff where defined.
+- **Event lifecycle**: streaming/polling connectors must test subscribe → emit → ack (if required).
+
+### 9.2 Mocking + Isolation (MANDATORY)
+
+- **No real API calls** in unit tests.
+- Use `wiremock` for HTTP mocks; use local in-process mock servers for WebSocket/SSE.
+- Deterministic fixtures only (no wall-clock or random data without fixed seeds).
+
+### 9.3 Property + Fuzz Targets (Connector Scope)
+
+Required property tests:
+- **Config parsing** never panics on arbitrary input.
+- **Serde round-trip** for connector config and operation input/output schemas.
+
+Required fuzz targets (per connector):
+- Configuration parsing
+- Protocol message parsing
+- Credential/token handling
+- Event payload parsing (where applicable)
+
+### 9.4 Security Regression Tests
+
+Each connector MUST include:
+- **No-secrets-in-logs** tests (deny raw tokens, API keys, auth headers).
+- **Credential zeroization** tests where credentials are stored in memory types.
+- **Network constraints enforcement** tests (deny-by-default egress).
+
+### 9.5 Connector Release Quality Gates
+
+In addition to Section 1 quality gates, connector releases MUST pass:
+- **Coverage**: >80% overall, >95% critical paths, >98% security-sensitive code.
+- **Mutation testing**: `cargo mutants` score >70%.
+- **Security audit**: `cargo audit --deny warnings`.
+- **E2E compliance harness**: mechanical compliance runner (static + dynamic).
+
+### 9.6 Mechanical Compliance (E2E)
+
+Every connector MUST pass the platform E2E/compliance harness:
+- Default deny (no capability token → denial + DecisionReceipt)
+- Zone binding enforcement
+- Sandbox/network constraints enforced
+- Tool descriptor validation (SEP-1382 compliance)
 - `lcov.info` - Coverage report

+**Telegram manifest contract (accepted):** the example above is the canonical "good" manifest.
+
+**Rejected examples (should fail validation):**
+
+```toml
+# BAD: network capability encodes host:port (must use network_constraints)
+[capabilities]
+required = ["network.outbound:api.telegram.org:443"]
+```
+
+```toml
+# BAD: streaming enabled but min_buffer_events = 0
+[event_caps]
+streaming = true
+replay = false
+min_buffer_events = 0
+```
+
+```toml
+# BAD: IP literal allowed and redirect policy too loose for Telegram
+[provides.operations."telegram.send_message"]
+network_constraints = { host_allow = ["1.2.3.4"], port_allow = [443], deny_ip_literals = false, max_redirects = 10 }
+```
+
+**Telegram event topics (contract):**
+- `telegram.message.new`
+- `telegram.message.edited`
+- `telegram.channel_post.new`
+- `telegram.channel_post.edited`
+- `telegram.callback_query`
+
+**Telegram event payload schema (JSON shape):**
+- `telegram.message.*` / `telegram.channel_post.*` payload:
+  - `message_id` (integer), `chat` (object), `from` (object), `date` (integer epoch seconds)
+  - `text` (string, optional), `caption` (string, optional)
+  - `has_photo` / `has_document` / `has_audio` / `has_video` / `has_voice` (boolean)
+  - `reply_to_message_id` (integer, optional), `message_thread_id` (integer, optional)
+- `telegram.callback_query` payload:
+  - `id` (string), `from` (object), `data` (string, optional), `chat_instance` (string)
+
+**Cursor semantics:** `update_id` is the monotonic cursor/seq source; replay is currently `false` and acks are not required.
+
+**Telegram capability families (contract):**
+- `telegram.messages.read` (Safe) — inbound updates / polling
+- `telegram.messages.send` (Risky) — send messages, answer callback queries
+- `telegram.media.read` (Safe) — fetch file metadata / download URL
+- `telegram.media.send` (Risky) — upload/send media
+- `telegram.chats.read` (Safe) — chat metadata
+- `telegram.members.read` (Safe) — member lists
+- `telegram.webhooks.manage` (Dangerous) — webhook configuration
